title: Decode-Strict Reject Vector
description: Grammar for vectors/v1/cbor/<profile>/hand-written/strict-decode-reject/*.json files. Each vector is a well-formed but non-canonical raw CBOR byte string that a conformant decode-strict implementation MUST reject with the given reason code (SPEC.md's `decode-strict reason codes` section). Profile applicability is implied by which profile's strict-decode-reject/ directory the file lives in, not by a field in the vector itself — same convention as logical-value.schema.json's encode vectors, which carry no profile field either. The canonical (ACCEPT) side of decode-strict conformance needs no new vectors: every existing hand-written encode vector's expected_hex doubles as a decode-strict ACCEPT input, expected to re-encode byte-identical to itself.

rule.description: One of SPEC.md's decode-strict reason codes. NON_SHORTEST_INT, UNSORTED_MAP_KEYS, INDEFINITE_LENGTH, DUPLICATE_KEY, NON_SHORTEST_FLOAT, NAN_PAYLOAD_VARIANT, TRAILING_BYTES, MULTIPLE_TOP_LEVEL_ITEMS, and UNKNOWN_TAG apply to both profiles (each gets a copy of the vector under both profiles' strict-decode-reject/ dirs, since the underlying rule and expected reason code are identical in rfc8949-profile-1 and dcbor-profile-<draft-version>). NON_NFC_STRING and UNREDUCED_NUMERIC are dcbor-only reason codes with no rfc8949 counterpart (rfc8949's P9 explicitly does not normalize string content, and rfc8949 has no numeric-reduction rule at all) — vectors for these two codes exist only under vectors/v1/cbor/dcbor/hand-written/strict-decode-reject/.

rationale.description: Why this specific byte sequence is well-formed CBOR yet non-canonical, and why it's a hard/worth-covering case.

input_hex.description: Well-formed (parseable) raw CBOR bytes that violate exactly one canonicity rule.

expected_reason.description: Must equal `rule` — kept as a separate field because this is the literal value the adapter's decode-strict CLI mode is contracted to print after `REJECT `, whereas `rule` is the vector's category tag (matching the `rule` field's role on encode vectors, e.g. `P6`, `bignum-rule`).
